𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Nine — Part Three

The Book of Tehillim

TEHILLIM

The Psalms — The Praises of Yisra'EL

Psalms 42 through 72

Book II — The Songs of the Sons of Qorah and Asaph

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

11Q5 (11QPsa) — Cave 11 — Major Psalms Scroll

4Q83 (4QPsa) — Cave 4 — Psalms 5-71 fragments

4Q84 (4QPsb) — Cave 4 — Psalms 42-49, 56-65

4Q86 (4QPsd) — Cave 4 — Psalms 48, 78, 81

4Q88 (4QPsf) — Cave 4 — Psalms 22, 69, 107, 109

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — All 150 Psalms plus Psalm 151

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

BOOK II — PSALMS 42 THROUGH 72 — COMPLETE

TEHILLIM — PSALM 42 Of the Sons of Qorah — A Maskil — As the Deer Pants for Water

1 As a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O Creator.

2 My soul thirsts for Creator, for the living Creator. When shall I come and appear before Creator?

3 My tears have been my food day and night, while they say to me all the day long, Where is your Creator?

4 These things I remember, as I pour out my soul: how I would go with the throng and lead them in procession to the house of Creator with glad shouts and songs of praise, a multitude keeping festival.

5 Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in Creator — for I shall again praise him, my salvation and my Creator.

6 My soul is cast down within me — therefore I remember you from the land of Yarden and Hermon, from Mount Mitzar.

7 Deep calls to deep at the roar of your waterfalls — all your breakers and your waves have gone over me.

8 By day YHWH commands his steadfast love, and at night his song is with me, a prayer to the Creator of my life.

9 I say to Creator my rock: Why have you forgotten me? Why do I go mourning because of the oppression of the enemy?

10 As with a deadly wound in my bones, my adversaries taunt me, while they say to me all the day long, Where is your Creator?

11 Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in Creator — for I shall again praise him, my salvation and my Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 42 opens Book II of the Tehillim and is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. Psalms 42 and 43 share a refrain and are understood as two parts of a single poem. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 42 in full. The opening image — as a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you — is one of the most beloved expressions of covenant longing in the Beta Israel tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 42. Psalms 42 and 43 together form the first psalm of Book II.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — deep calls to deep at the roar of your waterfalls — all your breakers and your waves have gone over me. The overwhelming waters are simultaneously YHWH's domain and the psalmist's experience of suffering. The refrain — why are you cast down, O my soul — is the psalmist addressing himself, commanding the soul to hope even when it does not feel hope. This is the covenant discipline of the interior life: commanding the soul to trust YHWH before the feelings of trust have arrived.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 43 Vindicate Me Against an Ungodly Nation — Send Out Your Light

1 Vindicate me, O Creator, and defend my cause against an ungodly people — from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me.

2 For you are the Creator in whom I take refuge — why have you rejected me? Why do I go about mourning because of the oppression of the enemy?

3 Send out your light and your truth — let them lead me — let them bring me to your holy hill and to your dwelling.

4 Then I will go to the altar of Creator, to Creator my exceeding joy, and I will praise you with the lyre, O Creator, my Creator.

5 Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in Creator — for I shall again praise him, my salvation and my Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 43 has no superscription and continues the lament of Psalm 42, attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The shared refrain confirms the two psalms are a single composition.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 43 in full. The petition — send out your light and your truth — let them lead me — is read as the covenant prayer for divine guidance in darkness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 43. The absence of a superscription confirms its unity with Psalm 42.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — send out your light and your truth — let them lead me — let them bring me to your holy hill. Light and truth are personified as YHWH's emissaries sent ahead to guide the psalmist to the sanctuary. The same imagery appears in Psalm 36:9 — in your light do we see light. Verse 4 — Creator my exceeding joy. The altar is the destination, and the joy found there is not in the ritual itself but in the presence of YHWH.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 44 Of the Sons of Qorah — A Maskil — We Have Not Forgotten You — Why Do You Sleep?

1 We have heard with our ears, O Creator — our fathers have told us, what deeds you performed in their days, in the days of old.

2 You with your own hand drove out the nations, but them you planted — you afflicted the peoples, but them you set free.

3 For not by their own sword did they win the land, nor did their own arm save them, but your right hand and your arm, and the light of your face, for you delighted in them.

4 You are my King, O Creator — ordain salvation for Yaakov.

5 Through you we push down our foes — through your name we tread down those who rise up against us.

6 For not in my bow do I trust, nor can my sword save me.

7 But you have saved us from our foes and have put to shame those who hate us.

8 In Creator we have boasted continually, and we will give thanks to your name forever.

9 But you have rejected and disgraced us and have not gone out with our armies.

10 You have made us turn back from the foe, and those who hate us have gotten spoil.

11 You have made us like sheep for slaughter and have scattered us among the nations.

12 You have sold your people for a trifle, demanding no high price for them.

13 You have made us the taunt of our neighbors, the derision and scorn of those around us.

14 You have made us a byword among the nations, a laughingstock among the peoples.

15 All day long my disgrace is before me, and shame has covered my face

16 At the sound of the taunter and reviler, at the sight of the enemy and the avenger.

17 All this has come upon us, though we have not forgotten you, and we have not been false to your covenant.

18 Our heart has not turned back, nor have our steps departed from your way.

19 Yet you have broken us in the place of jackals and covered us with the shadow of death.

20 If we had forgotten the name of our Creator or spread out our hands to a foreign god,

21 Would not Creator discover this? For he knows the secrets of the heart.

22 Yet for your sake we are killed all the day long — we are regarded as sheep to be slaughtered.

23 Awake — why are you sleeping, O YHWH? Rouse yourself — do not reject us forever.

24 Why do you hide your face? Why do you forget our affliction and oppression?

25 For our soul is bowed down to the dust — our belly clings to the ground.

26 Rise up — come to our help — redeem us for the sake of your steadfast love.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 44 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is a communal lament in a time of national defeat despite covenant faithfulness. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 44 in full. The psalm is read as the paradigmatic prayer of the covenant community in exile — innocent suffering, covenant faithfulness maintained, and urgent appeal to YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 44. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — all this has come upon us, though we have not forgotten you, and we have not been false to your covenant. Psalm 44 is one of the most theologically daring psalms — it denies that the suffering is punishment for covenant breaking. Verse 22 — for your sake we are killed all the day long. Suffering not because of sin but because of covenant loyalty. Verse 23 — awake, why are you sleeping, O YHWH? The boldness of this address is covenant intimacy pushed to its limit.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 45 Of the Sons of Qorah — A Maskil — A Love Song — The Royal Wedding Psalm

1 My heart overflows with a pleasing theme — I address my verses to the king — my tongue is like the pen of a ready scribe.

2 You are the most handsome of the sons of men — grace is poured upon your lips — therefore Creator has blessed you forever.

3 Gird your sword on your thigh, O mighty one, in your splendor and majesty.

4 In your majesty ride out victoriously for the cause of truth and meekness and righteousness — let your right hand teach you awesome deeds.

5 Your arrows are sharp in the heart of the king's enemies — the peoples fall under you.

6 Your throne, O Creator, is forever and ever — the scepter of your kingdom is a scepter of uprightness.

7 You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness — therefore Creator, your Creator, has anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond your companions.

8 Your robes are all fragrant with myrrh and aloes and cassia — from ivory palaces stringed instruments make you glad.

9 Daughters of kings are among your ladies of honor — at your right hand stands the queen in gold of Ophir.

10 Hear, O daughter, and consider, and incline your ear — forget your people and your father's house,

11 And the king will desire your beauty. Since he is your sovereign, bow to him.

12 The people of Tyre will seek your favor with gifts — the richest of the people will plead your favor.

13 All glorious is the princess in her chamber, with robes interwoven with gold.

14 In many-colored robes she is led to the king, with her virgin companions following behind her.

15 With joy and gladness they are led along as they enter the palace of the king.

16 In place of your fathers shall be your sons — you will make them princes in all the earth.

17 I will cause your name to be remembered in all generations — therefore nations will praise you forever and ever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 45 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is a royal wedding poem — the only explicit love poem in the canonical Tehillim. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 45 in full. The psalm is read in the Beta Israel tradition as both a literal royal wedding poem and an allegorical expression of the relationship between YHWH and his covenant people.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 45. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — your throne, O Creator, is forever and ever — the scepter of your kingdom is a scepter of uprightness. The king is addressed as Creator — the only place in the Tehillim where a human king receives this direct address. The verse declares that YHWH's throne is exercised through the anointed king — the Davidic covenant of 2 Shemu'el 7. Verse 7 — you have loved righteousness and hated wickedness — therefore Creator, your Creator, has anointed you. The anointing is the consequence of the king's moral character.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 46 Of the Sons of Qorah — A Song — Creator Is Our Refuge and Strength — Be Still

1 Creator is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble.

2 Therefore we will not fear though the earth gives way, though the mountains be moved into the heart of the sea,

3 Though its waters roar and foam, though the mountains tremble at its swelling.

4 There is a river whose streams make glad the city of Creator, the holy habitation of the Most High.

5 Creator is in the midst of her — she shall not be moved — Creator will help her when morning dawns.

6 The nations rage, the kingdoms totter — he utters his voice, the earth melts.

7 YHWH of hosts is with us — the Creator of Yaakov is our fortress.

8 Come, behold the works of YHWH, how he has brought desolations on the earth.

9 He makes wars cease to the end of the earth — he breaks the bow and shatters the spear — he burns the chariots with fire.

10 Be still, and know that I am Creator — I will be exalted among the nations, I will be exalted in the earth.

11 YHWH of hosts is with us — the Creator of Yaakov is our fortress.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 46 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The War Scroll (1QM) quotes verse 9 — he makes wars cease — as the theological conclusion of the final battle. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 46 in full. Verse 10 — be still, and know that I am Creator — is one of the most cited covenant declarations in the Beta Israel tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 46. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — be still, and know that I am Creator. The Hebrew raphah — be still — means to relax the grip, to cease striving. The command is issued in the context of cosmic upheaval — mountains moving, nations raging, the earth melting. The appropriate response is not action but surrender to the knowledge that YHWH is Creator. Verse 9 is quoted in the War Scroll Column XVII as the declaration that YHWH fights the final war so that war itself may end.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 47 Of the Sons of Qorah — Clap Your Hands — Creator Is King Over All the Earth

1 Clap your hands, all peoples — shout to Creator with loud songs of joy.

2 For YHWH, the Most High, is to be feared — a great king over all the earth.

3 He subdued peoples under us, and nations under our feet.

4 He chose our heritage for us, the pride of Yaakov whom he loves.

5 Creator has gone up with a shout, YHWH with the sound of a trumpet.

6 Sing praises to Creator, sing praises — sing praises to our King, sing praises.

7 For Creator is the King of all the earth — sing praises with a maskil.

8 Creator reigns over the nations — Creator sits on his holy throne.

9 The princes of the peoples gather as the people of the Creator of Avraham — for the shields of the earth belong to Creator — he is highly exalted.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 47 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is an enthronement psalm celebrating YHWH's kingship over all the earth. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 47 in full. Verse 5 — Creator has gone up with a shout, YHWH with the sound of a trumpet — is read in connection with the Ark's procession and YHWH's enthronement in the sanctuary.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 47. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — the princes of the peoples gather as the people of the Creator of Avraham. The Abrahamic covenant in Bereshit 17:5 — I have made you the father of a multitude of nations — finds its fulfillment here: the nations' princes gather under the sovereignty of YHWH. The covenant with Avraham was never merely ethnic — it was always oriented toward the nations. Psalm 47 is the Tehillim's celebration of that universal scope.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 48 Of the Sons of Qorah — A Song — Great Is YHWH in the City of Our Creator

1 Great is YHWH and greatly to be praised in the city of our Creator — his holy mountain,

2 Beautiful in elevation, is the joy of all the earth — Mount Tziyon, in the far north, the city of the great King.

3 Within her citadels Creator has made himself known as a fortress.

4 For behold, the kings assembled — they came on together.

5 As soon as they saw it, they were astounded — they were in panic, they took to flight.

6 Trembling took hold of them there — anguish as of a woman in labor.

7 By the east wind you shattered the ships of Tarshish.

8 As we have heard, so have we seen in the city of YHWH of hosts, in the city of our Creator — Creator will establish it forever.

9 We have thought on your steadfast love, O Creator, in the midst of your temple.

10 As your name, O Creator, so your praise reaches to the ends of the earth — your right hand is filled with righteousness.

11 Let Mount Tziyon be glad — let the daughters of Yehudah rejoice because of your judgments.

12 Walk about Tziyon, go around her, number her towers,

13 Consider well her ramparts, go through her citadels, that you may tell the next generation

14 That this is Creator, our Creator forever and ever. He will guide us forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 48 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) and 4QPsd (4Q86) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is a Tziyon hymn celebrating the city of YHWH as the center of his presence and protection. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 48 in full. The tour of Tziyon in verses 12-13 is read as the covenant community's act of faithful witness to YHWH's protection of the holy city.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 48. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14 — this is Creator, our Creator forever and ever. He will guide us forever. The tour of the city ends not with the city's walls but with the theological declaration: Creator guides forever. The physical walls of Tziyon are the visible sign of an invisible reality. Verse 8 — as we have heard, so have we seen. The covenant community's testimony is experiential: the promises received through covenant tradition are confirmed by direct experience.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 49 Of the Sons of Qorah — A Maskil — Wealth Cannot Ransom — Creator Will Ransom My Soul

1 Hear this, all peoples — give ear, all inhabitants of the world,

2 Both low and high, rich and poor together.

3 My mouth shall speak wisdom — the meditation of my heart shall be understanding.

4 I will incline my ear to a proverb — I will solve my riddle to the music of the lyre.

5 Why should I fear in times of trouble, when the iniquity of those who cheat me surrounds me,

6 Those who trust in their wealth and boast of the abundance of their riches?

7 Truly no man can ransom another, or give to Creator the price of his life,

8 For the ransom of their life is costly and can never suffice,

9 That he should live on forever and never see the pit.

10 For he sees that even the wise die — the fool and the stupid alike must perish and leave their wealth to others.

11 Their graves are their homes forever, their dwelling places to all generations, though they called lands by their own names.

12 Man in his pomp will not remain — he is like the beasts that perish.

13 This is the path of those who have foolish confidence — and after them others approve of their boasts.

14 Like sheep they are appointed for Sheol — death shall be their shepherd, and the upright shall rule over them in the morning. Their form shall be consumed in Sheol, with no place to dwell.

15 But Creator will ransom my soul from the power of Sheol, for he will receive me.

16 Be not afraid when a man becomes rich, when the glory of his house increases.

17 For when he dies he will carry nothing away — his glory will not go down after him.

18 For though, while he lives, he counts himself blessed — and though you get praise when you do well for yourself —

19 His soul will go to the generation of his fathers, who will never again see light.

20 Man in his pomp yet without understanding is like the beasts that perish.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 49 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is a wisdom psalm addressed to all peoples on the universal human condition before death. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 49 in full. Verse 15 — Creator will ransom my soul from the power of Sheol, for he will receive me — is read as one of the clearest statements of resurrection faith in the Tehillim.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 49. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15 — but Creator will ransom my soul from the power of Sheol, for he will receive me. Against the impossibility of human ransom — verses 7-8 — stands the covenant affirmation: YHWH will ransom. The word receive — laqach — is the same word used of Chanokh in Bereshit 5:24 — Creator took him. The psalmist expects what Chanokh experienced: to be received by YHWH beyond the reach of death.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 50 Of Asaph — Creator Calls the Earth to Judgment — Offer a Sacrifice of Thanksgiving

1 The Mighty One, Creator Creator, speaks and summons the earth from the rising of the sun to its setting.

2 Out of Tziyon, the perfection of beauty, Creator shines forth.

3 Our Creator comes — he does not keep silence — before him is a devouring fire, around him a mighty tempest.

4 He calls to the heavens above and to the earth, that he may judge his people.

5 Gather to me my faithful ones, who made a covenant with me by sacrifice.

6 The heavens declare his righteousness, for Creator himself is judge.

7 Hear, O my people, and I will speak — O Yisra'EL, I will testify against you. I am Creator, your Creator.

8 Not for your sacrifices do I rebuke you — your burnt offerings are continually before me.

9 I will not accept a bull from your house or goats from your folds.

10 For every beast of the forest is mine, the cattle on a thousand hills.

11 I know all the birds of the hills, and all that moves in the field is mine.

12 If I were hungry, I would not tell you, for the world and its fullness are mine.

13 Do I eat the flesh of bulls or drink the blood of goats?

14 Offer to Creator a sacrifice of thanksgiving, and perform your vows to the Most High,

15 And call upon me in the day of trouble — I will deliver you, and you shall glorify me.

16 But to the wicked Creator says: What right have you to recite my statutes or take my covenant on your lips?

17 For you hate discipline, and you cast my words behind you.

18 If you see a thief, you are pleased with him, and you keep company with adulterers.

19 You give your mouth free rein for evil, and your tongue frames deceit.

20 You sit and speak against your brother — you slander your own mother's son.

21 These things you have done, and I have been silent — you thought that I was one like yourself. But now I rebuke you and lay the charge before you.

22 Mark this, then, you who forget Creator, lest I tear you apart, and there be none to deliver.

23 The one who offers thanksgiving as his sacrifice glorifies me — to one who orders his way rightly I will show the salvation of Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 50 is the first psalm attributed to Asaph and opens a new collection within Book II. It is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 50 in full. The covenant lawsuit — YHWH calling heaven and earth as witnesses — is read as the foundational template for the prophetic tradition's use of the same structure in Yesha'yahu 1 and Mikkah 6.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 50. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 12-13 — the world and its fullness are mine. Do I eat the flesh of bulls or drink the blood of goats? Creator needs nothing from the sacrifice. Verses 14-15 redefine true sacrifice: offer thanksgiving, perform vows, call upon me in trouble. Verse 21 — you thought that I was one like yourself. The wicked project their own character onto YHWH — they assume his silence is approval. The silence was restraint, not assent.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 51 Of Dawid — When Natan the Prophet Came to Him — Create in Me a Clean Heart

1 Have mercy on me, O Creator, according to your steadfast love — according to your abundant mercy blot out my transgressions.

2 Wash me thoroughly from my iniquity, and cleanse me from my sin.

3 For I know my transgressions, and my sin is ever before me.

4 Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight, so that you may be justified in your words and blameless in your judgment.

5 Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin did my mother conceive me.

6 Behold, you delight in truth in the inward being, and you teach me wisdom in the secret heart.

7 Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean — wash me, and I shall be whiter than snow.

8 Let me hear joy and gladness — let the bones that you have broken rejoice.

9 Hide your face from my sins, and blot out all my iniquities.

10 Create in me a clean heart, O Creator, and renew a right spirit within me.

11 Cast me not away from your presence, and take not your Holy Spirit from me.

12 Restore to me the joy of your salvation, and uphold me with a willing spirit.

13 Then I will teach transgressors your ways, and sinners will return to you.

14 Deliver me from bloodguiltiness, O Creator, O Creator of my salvation, and my tongue will sing aloud of your righteousness.

15 O YHWH, open my lips, and my mouth will declare your praise.

16 For you will not delight in sacrifice, or I would give it — you will not be pleased with a burnt offering.

17 The sacrifices of Creator are a broken spirit — a broken and contrite heart, O Creator, you will not despise.

18 Do good to Tziyon in your good pleasure — build up the walls of Yerushalaym.

19 Then will you delight in right sacrifices, in burnt offerings and whole burnt offerings — then bulls will be offered on your altar.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 51 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription connects the psalm to Dawid's response after Natan the prophet confronted him. It is the most celebrated of the seven penitential psalms. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 51 in full. Verse 10 — create in me a clean heart, O Creator — is one of the most cited covenant prayers in the Beta Israel tradition and is used in liturgical contexts of confession and renewal.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 51. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — create in me a clean heart, O Creator. The word create — bara — is the same word used in Bereshit 1:1. The psalmist asks for an act of new creation within himself. The clean heart is not a reformed heart — it is a created heart. Only YHWH creates. Verse 17 — the sacrifices of Creator are a broken spirit — a broken and contrite heart, O Creator, you will not despise. This is Psalm 40:6-8 and Psalm 50:14 converging: the acceptable sacrifice is the crushed and surrendered self before YHWH.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 52 Of Dawid — A Maskil — Why Do You Boast of Evil — The Green Olive Tree

1 Why do you boast of evil, O mighty man? The steadfast love of Creator endures all the day.

2 Your tongue plots destruction, like a sharp razor, you worker of deceit.

3 You love evil more than good, and lying more than speaking what is right.

4 You love all words that devour, O deceitful tongue.

5 But Creator will break you down forever — he will snatch and tear you from your tent — he will uproot you from the land of the living.

6 The righteous shall see and fear, and shall laugh at him, saying,

7 See the man who would not make Creator his refuge, but trusted in the abundance of his riches and sought refuge in his own destruction.

8 But I am like a green olive tree in the house of Creator — I trust in the steadfast love of Creator forever and ever.

9 I will thank you forever, because you have done it — I will wait for your name, for it is good, in the presence of the godly.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 52 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription connects the psalm to Doeg the Edomite reporting to Sha'ul. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 52 in full. Verse 8 — I am like a green olive tree in the house of Creator — is read as the covenant person's self-understanding: rooted in YHWH's house, sustained by his steadfast love.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 52. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — I am like a green olive tree in the house of Creator. Against the destruction of the mighty man who trusted in wealth — verses 5-7 — the psalmist places himself as a green olive tree: rooted, living, producing. The olive tree is one of the central covenant images of the land — Devarim 8:8. The green olive tree in the house of Creator is the covenant person whose roots go into YHWH's own presence. No external destruction can uproot what is rooted in YHWH.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 53 Of Dawid — A Maskil — The Fool Says There Is No Creator — Variant of Psalm 14

1 The fool says in his heart, There is no Creator. They are corrupt, doing abominable iniquity — there is none who does good.

2 Creator looks down from heaven on the children of man to see if there are any who understand, who seek after Creator.

3 They have all fallen away — together they have become corrupt — there is none who does good, not even one.

4 Have those who work evil no knowledge, who eat up my people as they eat bread, and do not call upon Creator?

5 There they are, in great terror, where there is no terror — for Creator scatters the bones of him who encamps against you — you put them to shame, for Creator has rejected them.

6 Oh, that salvation for Yisra'EL would come out of Tziyon — when Creator restores the fortunes of his people, let Yaakov rejoice, let Yisra'EL be glad.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 53 is the near-duplicate of Psalm 14, using Creator — Elohim — where Psalm 14 uses YHWH. It is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The variant in verse 5 differs significantly from Psalm 14:5-6, confirming independent transmission.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 53 in full. The existence of both Psalm 14 and Psalm 53 reflects the two major divine name traditions preserved side by side in the covenant canon.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] Psalm 53 shows a significant variant from Psalm 14 in verse 5. Where Psalm 14:5-6 reads: Creator is with the generation of the righteous — Psalm 53:5 reads: Creator scatters the bones of him who encamps against you. The DSS preserves both versions, confirming their independent transmission. In this compilation YHWH is used as the primary divine name throughout.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 53 and Psalm 14 together demonstrate the ancient practice of preserving variant versions of sacred texts within the same collection. The Qumran community preserved multiple manuscript traditions side by side — the methodology reflected in the comparative notes of this compilation. The variant in verse 5 of Psalm 53 intensifies the judgment language: Creator scatters the bones of those who attack his people.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 54 Of Dawid — A Maskil — Save Me by Your Name

1 O Creator, save me by your name, and vindicate me by your might.

2 O Creator, hear my prayer — give ear to the words of my mouth.

3 For strangers have risen against me — ruthless men seek my life — they do not set Creator before themselves.

4 Behold, Creator is my helper — YHWH is the upholder of my life.

5 He will return the evil to my enemies — in your faithfulness put an end to them.

6 With a freewill offering I will sacrifice to you — I will give thanks to your name, YHWH, for it is good.

7 For he has delivered me from every trouble, and my eye has looked in triumph on my enemies.

8 I will give thanks to your name, YHWH, for it is good — for he has delivered me from every trouble and my eye has seen the defeat of my enemies.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 54 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription connects the psalm to the Ziphites' betrayal of Dawid to Sha'ul. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 54 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 54. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — save me by your name. The name of YHWH is itself the saving power. The theology of the divine name as the active presence of YHWH runs through the entire Torah: YHWH's name is placed on his people at the Priestly Blessing of Bamidbar 6:27. The banners and trumpets of the War Scroll are inscribed with YHWH's name because the name carries his presence. Psalm 54 prays that the name itself act as the saving agent.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 55 Of Dawid — A Maskil — Cast Your Burden on YHWH — Betrayal by a Close Friend

1 Give ear to my prayer, O Creator, and hide not yourself from my plea for mercy.

2 Attend to me, and answer me — I am restless in my complaint and I moan,

3 Because of the noise of the enemy, because of the oppression of the wicked. For they drop trouble upon me, and in anger they bear a grudge against me.

4 My heart is in anguish within me — the terrors of death have fallen upon me.

5 Fear and trembling come upon me, and horror overwhelms me.

6 And I say, Oh, that I had wings like a dove — I would fly away and be at rest.

7 Yes, I would wander far away — I would lodge in the wilderness.

8 I would hurry to find a shelter from the raging wind and tempest.

9 Destroy, O YHWH, divide their tongues — for I see violence and strife in the city.

10 Day and night they go around it on its walls, and iniquity and trouble are within it.

11 Ruin is in its midst — oppression and fraud do not depart from its marketplace.

12 For it is not an enemy who taunts me — then I could bear it — it is not an adversary who deals insolently with me — then I could hide from him.

13 But it is you, a man, my equal, my companion, my familiar friend.

14 We used to take sweet counsel together — within Creator's house we walked in the throng.

15 Let death steal over them — let them go down to Sheol alive — for evil is in their dwelling place and in their heart.

16 But I call to Creator, and YHWH will save me.

17 Evening and morning and at noon I utter my complaint and moan, and he hears my voice.

18 He redeems my soul in safety from the battle that I wage, for many are arrayed against me.

19 Creator will give ear and humble them, he who is enthroned from of old — because they do not change and do not fear Creator.

20 My companion stretched out his hand against his friends — he violated his covenant.

21 His speech was smooth as butter, yet war was in his heart — his words were softer than oil, yet they were drawn swords.

22 Cast your burden on YHWH, and he will sustain you — he will never permit the righteous to be moved.

23 But you, O Creator, will cast them down into the pit of destruction — men of blood and treachery shall not live out half their days. But I will trust in you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 55 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm describes betrayal by a close companion — the same theme that connects to Psalm 41:9 and the Qumran Damascus Document's warnings about betrayal within the covenant community. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 55 in full. Verse 22 — cast your burden on YHWH, and he will sustain you — is one of the most cited practical covenant promises in the Beta Israel tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 55. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 13-14 — but it is you, a man, my equal, my companion, my familiar friend — we used to take sweet counsel together — within Creator's house we walked in the throng. The betrayer is not a stranger or an enemy — he is a covenant companion. They walked together in the house of Creator. They shared sweet counsel — the same sod of Psalm 25:14. The devastation of betrayal by a covenant brother is the deepest human wound in the Tehillim. Verse 22 — cast your burden on YHWH, and he will sustain you. The response to betrayal by a man is trust in YHWH.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 56 Of Dawid — A Miktam — Be Gracious to Me, O Creator — In Creator I Trust Without Fear

1 Be gracious to me, O Creator, for man tramples on me — all day long an attacker oppresses me.

2 My enemies trample on me all day long, for many attack me proudly.

3 When I am afraid, I put my trust in you.

4 In Creator, whose word I praise — in Creator I trust — I shall not be afraid. What can flesh do to me?

5 All day long they injure my cause — all their thoughts are against me for evil.

6 They stir up strife, they lurk — they watch my steps, as they have waited for my life.

7 For their crime will they escape? In wrath cast down the peoples, O Creator.

8 You have kept count of my tossings — put my tears in your bottle. Are they not in your book?

9 Then my enemies will turn back in the day when I call. This I know, that Creator is for me.

10 In Creator, whose word I praise — in YHWH, whose word I praise —

11 In Creator I trust — I shall not be afraid. What can man do to me?

12 I must perform my vows to you, O Creator — I will render thank offerings to you.

13 For you have delivered my soul from death, yes, my feet from falling, that I may walk before Creator in the light of life.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 56 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription connects the psalm to Dawid's capture by the Philistines in Gath. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 56 in full. Verse 8 — you have kept count of my tossings — put my tears in your bottle. Are they not in your book? — is read as the declaration that every suffering of the covenant person is recorded before YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 56. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — you have kept count of my tossings — put my tears in your bottle. Are they not in your book? The heavenly tablets of the Chanokh literature record all human deeds. Psalm 56 declares that YHWH also records tears — the suffering of the covenant person is not forgotten but preserved before YHWH. Every tear is noted. The bottle of tears and the book of tossings are the intimate record-keeping of a Creator who misses nothing of the covenant person's experience. Verse 3 — when I am afraid, I put my trust in you. Not when fear has gone — when I am afraid. The trust is placed in the moment of fear, not after it passes.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 57 Of Dawid — A Miktam — Be Merciful to Me, O Creator — My Heart Is Steadfast

1 Be merciful to me, O Creator, be merciful to me, for in you my soul takes refuge — in the shadow of your wings I will take refuge, till the storms of destruction pass by.

2 I cry out to Creator Most High, to Creator who fulfills his purpose for me.

3 He will send from heaven and save me — he will put to shame him who tramples on me. Creator will send out his steadfast love and his faithfulness.

4 My soul is in the midst of lions — I lie down amid fiery beasts — the children of man, whose teeth are spears and arrows, whose tongues are sharp swords.

5 Be exalted, O Creator, above the heavens — let your glory be over all the earth.

6 They set a net for my steps — my soul was bowed down. They dug a pit in my way, but they have fallen into it themselves.

7 My heart is steadfast, O Creator, my heart is steadfast — I will sing and make melody.

8 Awake, my glory — awake, O harp and lyre — I will awake the dawn.

9 I will give thanks to you, O YHWH, among the peoples — I will sing praises to you among the nations.

10 For your steadfast love is great to the heavens — your faithfulness to the clouds.

11 Be exalted, O Creator, above the heavens — let your glory be over all the earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 57 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription connects the psalm to Dawid's hiding in the cave from Sha'ul. The refrain of verse 5 and verse 11 — be exalted, O Creator, above the heavens — frames the psalm. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 57 in full. Verse 1 — in the shadow of your wings I will take refuge, till the storms of destruction pass by — is read as the definitive expression of covenant shelter: YHWH's wings as the covering through the worst storms.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 57. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — my heart is steadfast, O Creator, my heart is steadfast. The double declaration of steadfastness — said twice — is the psalmist's covenant commitment spoken against the background of lions and sharp swords and nets. The steadfastness is not a feeling — it is a declaration made by the will in the moment of threat. Verse 8 — I will awake the dawn. The psalmist does not wait for the dawn to arrive — he wakes it himself with the harp and lyre. Praise precedes the dawn. The song is sung before the light comes.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 58 Of Dawid — A Miktam — Do You Indeed Decree What Is Right — YHWH Judges the Earth

1 Do you indeed decree what is right, you gods? Do you judge the children of man uprightly?

2 No, in your hearts you devise wrongs — your hands deal out violence on earth.

3 The wicked are estranged from the womb — they go astray from birth, speaking lies.

4 They have venom like the venom of a serpent, like the deaf adder that stops its ear,

5 So that it does not hear the voice of charmers or of the cunning enchanter.

6 O Creator, break the teeth in their mouths — tear out the fangs of the young lions, O YHWH.

7 Let them vanish like water that runs away — when he aims his arrows, let them be blunted.

8 Let them be like the snail that dissolves into slime, like the stillborn child who never sees the sun.

9 Sooner than your pots can feel the heat of thorns, whether green or ablaze, may he sweep them away.

10 The righteous will rejoice when he sees the vengeance — he will bathe his feet in the blood of the wicked.

11 Mankind will say, Surely there is a reward for the righteous — surely there is a Creator who judges on earth.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 58 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm opens with a challenge to unjust rulers or divine beings who pervert justice. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 58 in full. Verse 11 — surely there is a Creator who judges on earth — is read as the foundational covenant declaration that justice is not an abstraction but an attribute of the living Creator who acts in history.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 58. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — do you indeed decree what is right, you gods? The question is addressed to rulers or possibly to the divine council — the heavenly beings whose role is to administer justice among the nations. The psalm accuses them of perverting justice from the womb — verse 3. Verse 11 — surely there is a Creator who judges on earth. The concluding declaration affirms what the opening question doubted: despite the perversion of justice by those entrusted with it, YHWH himself judges the earth. Human and cosmic injustice will not have the last word.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 59 Of Dawid — A Miktam — Deliver Me From My Enemies — My Strength, I Will Watch for You

1 Deliver me from my enemies, O my Creator — protect me from those who rise up against me.

2 Deliver me from those who work evil, and save me from bloodthirsty men.

3 For behold, they lie in wait for my life — fierce men stir up strife against me. For no transgression or sin of mine, O YHWH,

4 For no fault of mine, they run and make ready. Awake, come to meet me, and see.

5 You, YHWH Creator of hosts, are Creator of Yisra'EL. Rouse yourself to punish all the nations — spare none of those who treacherously plot evil.

6 Each evening they come back, howling like dogs and prowling about the city.

7 There they are, bellowing with their mouths with swords in their lips — for Who, they think, will hear us?

8 But you, O YHWH, laugh at them — you hold all the nations in derision.

9 O my Strength, I will watch for you, for you, O Creator, are my fortress.

10 My Creator in his steadfast love will meet me — Creator will let me look in triumph on my enemies.

11 Kill them not, lest my people forget — make them totter by your power and bring them down, O YHWH, our shield.

12 For the sin of their mouths, the words of their lips, let them be trapped in their pride. For the cursing and lies that they utter,

13 Consume them in wrath — consume them till they are no more, that they may know that Creator rules over Yaakov to the ends of the earth.

14 Each evening they come back, howling like dogs and prowling about the city.

15 They wander about for food and growl if they do not get their fill.

16 But I will sing of your strength — I will sing aloud of your steadfast love in the morning. For you have been to me a fortress and a refuge in the day of my distress.

17 O my Strength, I will sing praises to you, for you, O Creator, are my fortress, the Creator who shows me steadfast love.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 59 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription connects the psalm to Sha'ul's men watching Dawid's house to kill him. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 59 in full. The repeated image of enemies prowling like dogs in the city — verses 6 and 14 — is read as the covenant community's experience of persistent threat from those who do not acknowledge YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 59. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — O my Strength, I will watch for you. The psalmist watches for YHWH — the same watchful waiting of Psalm 130:6 — my soul waits for YHWH more than watchmen for the morning. The watch is active, expectant, directed toward YHWH rather than away from the enemy. Verse 16 — I will sing of your strength — I will sing aloud of your steadfast love in the morning. The psalm that began in darkness — enemies prowling at evening — ends in the morning song of steadfast love. The night is the frame; the morning is the point.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 60 Of Dawid — A Miktam — You Have Rejected Us — Give Us Help Against the Foe

1 O Creator, you have rejected us, broken our defenses — you have been angry — oh, restore us.

2 You have made the land to quake — you have torn it open — repair its breaches, for it totters.

3 You have made your people see hard things — you have given us wine to drink that made us stagger.

4 You have set up a banner for those who fear you, that they may flee to it from the bow.

5 That your beloved ones may be delivered, give salvation by your right hand and answer us.

6 Creator has spoken in his holiness: With exultation I will divide up Shekhem and portion out the Vale of Sukkot.

7 Gile'ad is mine — Menasheh is mine — Efrayim is my helmet — Yehudah is my scepter.

8 Moav is my washbasin — upon Edom I cast my shoe — over Peleshet I shout in triumph.

9 Who will bring me to the fortified city? Who will lead me to Edom?

10 Have you not rejected us, O Creator? You do not go out, O Creator, with our armies.

11 Oh, grant us help against the foe, for vain is the salvation of man.

12 With Creator we shall do valiantly — it is he who will tread down our foes.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 60 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription connects the psalm to Dawid's wars. The geographical survey of the covenant land in verses 6-8 reflects the ancient boundaries of the territory promised to Yisra'EL. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 60 in full. The psalm is read as the covenant community's prayer in a time of military defeat — YHWH has been angry, the land is shaken, but his sovereignty over all the nations remains.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 60. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — with Creator we shall do valiantly — it is he who will tread down our foes. After the complaint of verses 1-3 — you have rejected us, you have been angry, you made us stagger — and the petition of verses 5-11 — grant us help, for vain is the salvation of man — the psalm closes with covenant confidence: not in human armies but in YHWH alone. This is the theology of the War Scroll: the battle belongs to YHWH. The Sons of Light win not by military superiority but by YHWH's action.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 61 Of Dawid — Hear My Cry, O Creator — Lead Me to the Rock That Is Higher Than I

1 Hear my cry, O Creator — listen to my prayer.

2 From the end of the earth I call to you when my heart is faint — lead me to the rock that is higher than I.

3 For you have been my refuge, a strong tower against the enemy.

4 Let me dwell in your tent forever — let me take refuge under the shelter of your wings.

5 For you, O Creator, have heard my vows — you have given me the heritage of those who fear your name.

6 Prolong the life of the king — may his years endure to all generations.

7 May he be enthroned forever before Creator — appoint steadfast love and faithfulness to watch over him.

8 So will I ever sing praises to your name, as I perform my vows day after day.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 61 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm opens as a personal lament from the end of the earth — exile or the extremity of suffering — and moves to a royal prayer. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 61 in full. Verse 2 — lead me to the rock that is higher than I — is read as the covenant prayer of the person who has reached the limits of human capacity and can only ask to be brought to YHWH himself.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 61. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — lead me to the rock that is higher than I. The rock that is higher than the psalmist is YHWH himself — not a strategy, not a human ally, not even a theological argument. The prayer is simply to be led to the one who is beyond human reach. The end of the earth — verse 2 — is the farthest point from the sanctuary, the greatest distance from YHWH's dwelling. Even from there, the cry reaches him. Even from there, he leads to the rock.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 62 Of Dawid — A Psalm — My Soul Waits for Creator Alone — Pour Out Your Heart Before Him

1 For Creator alone my soul waits in silence — from him comes my salvation.

2 He alone is my rock and my salvation, my fortress — I shall not be greatly shaken.

3 How long will all of you attack a man to batter him, like a leaning wall, a tottering fence?

4 They only plan to thrust him down from his high position. They take pleasure in falsehood. They bless with their mouths, but inwardly they curse.

5 For Creator alone, O my soul, wait in silence, for my hope is from him.

6 He only is my rock and my salvation, my fortress — I shall not be shaken.

7 On Creator rests my salvation and my glory — my mighty rock, my refuge is Creator.

8 Trust in him at all times, O people — pour out your heart before him — Creator is a refuge for us.

9 Those of low estate are but a breath — those of high estate are a delusion — in the balances they go up — they are together lighter than a breath.

10 Put no trust in extortion — set no vain hopes on robbery — if riches increase, set not your heart on them.

11 Once Creator has spoken — twice have I heard this: that power belongs to Creator,

12 And that to you, O YHWH, belongs steadfast love. For you will render to a man according to his work.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 62 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm's structure is built around the word only — Hebrew ak — appearing five times to emphasize exclusive dependence on YHWH. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 62 in full. Verse 8 — pour out your heart before him — Creator is a refuge for us — is read as the invitation to complete transparency before YHWH in prayer.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 62. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — for Creator alone my soul waits in silence. The silence is not passive but focused — all the noise of threat and counter-threat stilled, the soul directed to YHWH alone. Verses 11-12 — once Creator has spoken, twice have I heard this: that power belongs to Creator, and that to you, YHWH, belongs steadfast love. The two covenant attributes — power and steadfast love — are declared as YHWH's exclusive possession. No human ruler, no earthly power has them in themselves. They belong to YHWH. The closing line — for you will render to a man according to his work — grounds the covenant theology in practical justice.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 63 Of Dawid — When He Was in the Wilderness of Yehudah — My Soul Thirsts for You

1 O Creator, you are my Creator — earnestly I seek you — my soul thirsts for you — my flesh faints for you, as in a dry and weary land where there is no water.

2 So I have looked upon you in the sanctuary, beholding your power and glory.

3 Because your steadfast love is better than life, my lips will praise you.

4 So I will bless you as long as I live — in your name I will lift up my hands.

5 My soul will be satisfied as with fat and rich food, and my mouth will praise you with joyful lips,

6 When I remember you upon my bed, and meditate on you in the watches of the night.

7 For you have been my help, and in the shadow of your wings I will sing for joy.

8 My soul clings to you — your right hand upholds me.

9 But those who seek to destroy my life shall go down into the depths of the earth.

10 They shall be given over to the power of the sword — they shall be a portion for jackals.

11 But the king shall rejoice in Creator — all who swear by him shall exult, for the mouths of liars will be stopped.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 63 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The superscription connects the psalm to Dawid's time in the wilderness of Yehudah. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 63 in full. Verse 3 — your steadfast love is better than life — is read as the definitive covenant valuation: YHWH's love exceeds even physical life in worth.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 63. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — your steadfast love is better than life. Life — chayim — is the highest earthly good in the Hebrew framework. Yet the psalmist declares YHWH's steadfast love to be better. The logic is covenant logic: life without YHWH's steadfast love is not life in its fullest sense. YHWH's love is the ground of life's meaning. Verse 6 — when I remember you upon my bed, and meditate on you in the watches of the night. The night watches are the hours of darkness when the mind has no external distraction — the time when either fear or YHWH fills the silence. The covenant person fills that silence with meditation on YHWH.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 64 Of Dawid — Hide Me From the Secret Plots of the Wicked — Creator Will Shoot His Arrow

1 Hear my voice, O Creator, in my complaint — preserve my life from dread of the enemy.

2 Hide me from the secret plots of the wicked, from the throng of evildoers,

3 Who whet their tongues like swords, who aim bitter words like arrows,

4 Shooting from ambush at the blameless, shooting at him suddenly and without fear.

5 They hold fast to their evil purpose — they talk of laying snares secretly, thinking, Who can see them?

6 They search out injustice, saying, We have accomplished a diligent search. For the inward mind and heart of a man are deep.

7 But Creator shoots his arrow at them — they are wounded suddenly.

8 They are brought to ruin, with their own tongues turned against them — all who see them will wag their heads.

9 Then all mankind fears — they tell what Creator has brought about and ponder what he has done.

10 Let the righteous one rejoice in YHWH and take refuge in him — let all the upright in heart exult.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 64 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm describes the conspiracy of the wicked who use speech as their weapon — their tongues like swords, their words like arrows. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 64 in full. The pattern of the psalm — the wicked shoot at the blameless with words, YHWH shoots his arrow at the wicked in return — is read as covenant justice enacted through the same instrument the wicked chose.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 64. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — but Creator shoots his arrow at them — they are wounded suddenly. The wicked have been shooting their arrows — bitter words — in ambush at the blameless — verse 3-4. YHWH responds with his own arrow. The instrument of evil becomes the instrument of judgment. The measure for measure principle runs through the Tehillim and the entire prophetic corpus. Verse 8 — with their own tongues turned against them. The weapon they chose — the tongue — is turned back on them.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 65 Of Dawid — A Song — Praise Is Due to You in Tziyon — You Crown the Year With Bounty

1 Praise is due to you, O Creator, in Tziyon, and to you shall vows be performed.

2 O you who hear prayer, to you shall all flesh come.

3 When iniquities prevail against me, you atone for our transgressions.

4 Blessed is the one you choose and bring near, to dwell in your courts — we shall be satisfied with the goodness of your house, the holiness of your temple.

5 By awesome deeds you answer us with righteousness, O Creator of our salvation, the hope of all the ends of the earth and of the farthest seas.

6 The one who by his strength established the mountains, being girded with might,

7 Who stills the roaring of the seas, the roaring of their waves, the tumult of the peoples,

8 So that those who dwell at the ends of the earth are in awe at your signs. You make the going out of the morning and the evening to shout for joy.

9 You visit the earth and water it — you greatly enrich it — the river of Creator is full of water — you provide their grain, for so you have prepared it.

10 You water its furrows abundantly, settling its ridges, softening it with showers, and blessing its growth.

11 You crown the year with your bounty — your wagon tracks overflow with abundance.

12 The pastures of the wilderness overflow — the hills gird themselves with joy.

13 The meadows clothe themselves with flocks — the valleys deck themselves with grain — they shout and sing together for joy.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 65 is attested in 4QPsb (4Q84) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is a harvest thanksgiving — celebrating YHWH's provision through the agricultural year. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 65 in full. The agricultural imagery of YHWH visiting the earth, watering it, crowning the year with bounty is read as the covenant promise of Devarim 11:14 fulfilled — YHWH giving rain in its season.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 65. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — you crown the year with your bounty — your wagon tracks overflow with abundance. The entire agricultural cycle is YHWH's activity: he visits and waters — verse 9 — he settles the ridges and softens with showers — verse 10 — he crowns the year — verse 11. The harvest is not the result of human agricultural skill but of YHWH's covenant provision. Verse 13 — the meadows clothe themselves with flocks — the valleys deck themselves with grain — they shout and sing together for joy. Creation itself participates in the harvest praise. The land worships its Creator by producing abundantly.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 66 A Song — A Psalm — Shout for Joy to Creator — Come and See What Creator Has Done

1 Shout for joy to Creator, all the earth.

2 Sing the glory of his name — give to him glorious praise.

3 Say to Creator, How awesome are your deeds — so great is your power that your enemies come cringing to you.

4 All the earth worships you and sings praises to you — they sing praises to your name.

5 Come and see what Creator has done — he is awesome in his deeds toward the children of man.

6 He turned the sea into dry land — they passed through the river on foot. There did we rejoice in him,

7 Who rules by his might forever, whose eyes keep watch on the nations — let not the rebellious exalt themselves.

8 Bless our Creator, O peoples — let the sound of his praise be heard,

9 Who has kept our soul among the living and has not let our feet slip.

10 For you, O Creator, have tested us — you have tried us as silver is tried.

11 You brought us into the net — you laid a crushing burden on our backs.

12 You let men ride over our heads — we went through fire and through water — yet you have brought us out to a place of abundance.

13 I will come into your house with burnt offerings — I will perform my vows to you,

14 That which my lips uttered and my mouth promised when I was in trouble.

15 I will offer to you burnt offerings of fattened animals, with the smoke of the sacrifice of rams — I will make an offering of bulls and goats.

16 Come and hear, all you who fear Creator, and I will tell what he has done for my soul.

17 I cried to him with my mouth, and high praise was on my tongue.

18 If I had cherished iniquity in my heart, YHWH would not have listened.

19 But truly Creator has listened — he has attended to the voice of my prayer.

20 Blessed be Creator, because he has not rejected my prayer or removed his steadfast love from me.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 66 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm opens as a universal call to praise and narrows to a personal testimony of answered prayer. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 66 in full. Verse 6 — he turned the sea into dry land — is read as the covenant community's foundational memory: the Exodus crossing is the paradigmatic act of YHWH's saving power on which all subsequent trust is built.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 66. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10-12 — you have tested us as silver is tried — you brought us into the net — you let men ride over our heads — we went through fire and through water — yet you have brought us out to a place of abundance. The testing is YHWH's own act. The net, the fire, the flood — all are within his sovereign governance of the covenant people's history. The word yet — verse 12 — is the pivot: all that suffering, all that testing, yet you brought us out. The testing is not abandoned but completed in abundance.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 67 A Psalm — A Song — May Creator Be Gracious to Us — Let the Peoples Praise You

1 May Creator be gracious to us and bless us and make his face to shine upon us,

2 That your way may be known on earth, your saving power among all nations.

3 Let the peoples praise you, O Creator — let all the peoples praise you.

4 Let the nations be glad and sing for joy, for you judge the peoples with equity and guide the nations upon earth.

5 Let the peoples praise you, O Creator — let all the peoples praise you.

6 The earth has yielded its increase — Creator, our Creator, shall bless us.

7 Creator shall bless us — let all the ends of the earth fear him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 67 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) with fragmentary coverage. The psalm is structured around the Priestly Blessing of Bamidbar 6:24-26 — the opening verse echoes the blessing directly. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 67 in full. The psalm is read as the covenant community's prayer that YHWH's blessing on Yisra'EL would become the means of blessing for all nations — the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 67. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1-2 — may Creator be gracious to us and bless us and make his face to shine upon us, that your way may be known on earth. The blessing is not an end in itself. The Priestly Blessing received by Yisra'EL has a purpose: that YHWH's way may be known among all nations. The blessing of the covenant people is the means of revealing YHWH to the world. The refrain — let the peoples praise you, O Creator, let all the peoples praise you — frames the psalm at verse 3 and verse 5, expressing the universal scope of the blessing.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 68 Of Dawid — A Psalm — A Song — Creator Arises — A Great Processional Hymn

1 Creator arises — his enemies are scattered — those who hate him flee before him.

2 As smoke is driven away, so you shall drive them away — as wax melts before fire, so the wicked shall perish before Creator.

3 But the righteous shall be glad — they shall exult before Creator — they shall be jubilant with joy.

4 Sing to Creator — sing praises to his name — lift up a song to him who rides through the deserts — his name is YHWH — exult before him.

5 Father of the fatherless and protector of widows is Creator in his holy habitation.

6 Creator settles the solitary in a home — he leads out the prisoners to prosperity, but the rebellious dwell in a parched land.

7 O Creator, when you went out before your people, when you marched through the wilderness,

8 The earth quaked — the heavens poured down rain before Creator — Sinai itself quaked before Creator, the Creator of Yisra'EL.

9 Rain in abundance, O Creator, you shed abroad — you restored your inheritance as it languished.

10 Your flock found a dwelling in it — in your goodness, O Creator, you provided for the needy.

11 YHWH gives the word — the women who announce the news are a great host.

12 The kings of the armies — they flee, they flee — the women at home divide the spoil.

13 Though you men lie among the sheepfolds — the wings of a dove covered with silver, its pinions with shimmering gold.

14 When the Almighty scatters kings there, snow falls on Tzalmon.

15 O mountain of Creator, mountain of Bashan — O many-peaked mountain, mountain of Bashan.

16 Why do you look with hatred, O many-peaked mountain, at the mount that Creator desired for his abode, yes, where YHWH will dwell forever?

17 The chariots of Creator are twice ten thousand, thousands upon thousands — YHWH is among them — Sinai is now in the sanctuary.

18 You ascended on high, leading a host of captives in your train and receiving gifts among men, even among the rebellious, that YHWH Creator may dwell there.

19 Blessed be YHWH, who daily bears us up — Creator is our salvation.

20 Our Creator is a Creator of salvation, and to YHWH, YHWH, belong deliverances from death.

21 But Creator will strike the heads of his enemies, the hairy crown of him who walks in his guilty ways.

22 YHWH said, I will bring them back from Bashan — I will bring them back from the depths of the sea,

23 That you may strike your feet in their blood, that the tongues of your dogs may have their portion from the foe.

24 Your procession is seen, O Creator — the procession of my Creator, my King, into the sanctuary.

25 The singers in front, the musicians last, between them virgins playing tambourines.

26 Bless Creator in the great congregation, YHWH, O you who are of Yisra'EL's fountain.

27 There is Binyamin, the least of them, in the lead — the princes of Yehudah in their throng — the princes of Zevulun, the princes of Naftali.

28 Summon your power, O Creator, the power, O Creator, that you have used for us.

29 Because of your temple at Yerushalaym kings shall bear gifts to you.

30 Rebuke the beasts that dwell among the reeds, the herd of bulls with the calves of the peoples. Trample underfoot those who lust after tribute — scatter the peoples who delight in war.

31 Nobles shall come from Egypt — Kush shall hasten to stretch out her hands to Creator.

32 O kingdoms of the earth, sing to Creator — sing praises to YHWH,

33 To him who rides in the heavens, the ancient heavens — behold, he sends out his voice, his mighty voice.

34 Ascribe power to Creator, whose majesty is over Yisra'EL, and whose power is in the skies.

35 Awesome is Creator from his sanctuary — the Creator of Yisra'EL — he is the one who gives power and strength to his people. Blessed be Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 68 is one of the longest and most complex psalms in the Tehillim, attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm is a great processional hymn celebrating YHWH's march from Sinai to Tziyon. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 68 in full. Verse 31 — Kush shall hasten to stretch out her hands to Creator — is of particular significance in the Beta Israel tradition, read as the prophetic declaration that the nations of northeast Africa will worship YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 68. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — father of the fatherless and protector of widows is Creator in his holy habitation. YHWH's cosmic power — riding through the deserts, scattering enemies — is held alongside his care for the most vulnerable: the orphan and the widow. Covenant power and covenant compassion are inseparable. Verse 17 — the chariots of Creator are twice ten thousand, thousands upon thousands — YHWH is among them — Sinai is now in the sanctuary. The Sinai theophany has moved. YHWH who appeared at Sinai now dwells in the sanctuary at Tziyon. The revelation of Sinai is permanently housed in the covenant community's worship.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 69 Of Dawid — Save Me, O Creator — The Waters Have Come Up to My Neck

1 Save me, O Creator — for the waters have come up to my neck.

2 I sink in deep mire, where there is no foothold — I have come into deep waters, and the flood sweeps over me.

3 I am weary with my crying out — my throat is parched. My eyes grow dim with waiting for my Creator.

4 More in number than the hairs of my head are those who hate me without cause — mighty are those who would destroy me, those who attack me with lies. What I did not steal must I now restore?

5 O Creator, you know my folly — the wrongs I have done are not hidden from you.

6 Let not those who hope in you be put to shame through me, O YHWH Creator of hosts — let not those who seek you be brought to dishonor through me, O Creator of Yisra'EL.

7 For it is for your sake that I have borne reproach, that dishonor has covered my face.

8 I have become a stranger to my brothers, an alien to my mother's sons.

9 For zeal for your house has consumed me, and the reproaches of those who reproach you have fallen on me.

10 When I wept and humbled my soul with fasting, it became my reproach.

11 When I made sackcloth my clothing, I became a byword to them.

12 I am the talk of those who sit in the gate, and the drunkards make songs about me.

13 But as for me, my prayer is to you, O YHWH. At an acceptable time, O Creator, in the abundance of your steadfast love answer me in your saving faithfulness.

14 Deliver me from sinking in the mire — let me be delivered from my enemies and from the deep waters.

15 Let not the flood sweep over me, or the deep swallow me up, or the pit close its mouth over me.

16 Answer me, YHWH, for your steadfast love is good — according to your abundant mercy, turn to me.

17 Hide not your face from your servant — for I am in distress — make haste to answer me.

18 Draw near to my soul, redeem it — ransom me because of my enemies.

19 You know my reproach, and my shame and my dishonor — my foes are all known to you.

20 Reproaches have broken my heart, so that I am in despair. I looked for pity, but there was none, and for comforters, but I found none.

21 They gave me poison for food, and for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink.

22 Let their own table before them become a snare — and when they are at peace, let it become a trap.

23 Let their eyes be darkened, so that they cannot see, and make their loins tremble continually.

24 Pour out your indignation upon them, and let your burning anger overtake them.

25 May their camp be a desolation — let no one dwell in their tents.

26 For they persecute him whom you have struck down, and they recount the pain of those you have wounded.

27 Add to them punishment upon punishment — may they have no acquittal from you.

28 Let them be blotted out of the book of the living — let them not be enrolled among the righteous.

29 But I am afflicted and in pain — let your salvation, O Creator, set me on high.

30 I will praise the name of Creator with a song — I will magnify him with thanksgiving.

31 This will please YHWH more than an ox or a bull with horns and hoofs.

32 When the humble see it they will be glad — you who seek Creator, let your hearts revive.

33 For YHWH hears the needy and does not despise his own people who are prisoners.

34 Let heaven and earth praise him, the seas and everything that moves in them.

35 For Creator will save Tziyon and build up the cities of Yehudah, and people shall dwell there and possess it.

36 The offspring of his servants shall inherit it, and those who love his name shall dwell in it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 69 is attested in 4QPsf (4Q88) from Cave 4 which preserves portions of this psalm. Verse 9 — zeal for your house has consumed me — and verses 21-22 are among the most cited passages from the Tehillim in the Second Temple period literature. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 69 in full. The psalm is read as one of the most complete portraits of righteous suffering in the entire Tehillim — the servant of YHWH bearing reproach because of covenant faithfulness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 69. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — zeal for your house has consumed me. The psalmist's suffering is specifically connected to covenant faithfulness — he bears reproach because he is consumed with zeal for YHWH's house. Verse 28 — let them be blotted out of the book of the living — let them not be enrolled among the righteous. The book of the living is the heavenly covenant record — the same heavenly tablets described in Chanokh 81 and the War Scroll. Those blotted from this book are cut off from the covenant community forever. Verses 35-36 close with the eschatological vision: Creator will save Tziyon, the offspring of his servants shall inherit it.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 70 Of Dawid — A Psalm for the Memorial Offering — Make Haste to Help Me

1 Make haste, O Creator, to deliver me — O YHWH, make haste to help me.

2 Let them be put to shame and confusion who seek my life — let them be turned back and brought to dishonor who delight in my hurt.

3 Let them turn back because of their shame who say, Aha, aha.

4 May all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you — may those who love your salvation say evermore, Creator is great.

5 But I am poor and needy — hasten to me, O Creator — you are my help and my deliverer — O YHWH, do not delay.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 70 is an abbreviated version of Psalm 40:13-17, attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm is marked as a memorial offering psalm. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 70 in full. The psalm is read as the urgent cry of the covenant person in immediate danger — stripped of all elaboration to the essential: make haste, O Creator.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 70. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 70 is the concentrated essence of Psalm 40:13-17. Its preservation as an independent psalm confirms the ancient liturgical practice of extracting key sections of longer psalms for specific uses — here for the memorial offering. Verse 5 — but I am poor and needy — hasten to me, O Creator — you are my help and my deliverer. The final verse combines poverty, urgency, and covenant confidence. The same three elements appear together throughout the Tehillim: the psalmist is poor and needy — he has no resources of his own — yet he knows YHWH is his help and deliverer.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 71 A Prayer of Old Age — Do Not Cast Me Off When I Am Old — I Have Hoped in You from My Youth

1 In you, O YHWH, do I take refuge — let me never be put to shame.

2 In your righteousness deliver me and rescue me — incline your ear to me, and save me.

3 Be to me a rock of refuge, to which I may continually come — you have given the command to save me, for you are my rock and my fortress.

4 Rescue me, O my Creator, from the hand of the wicked, from the grasp of the unjust and cruel man.

5 For you, O YHWH, are my hope — my trust, O YHWH, from my youth.

6 Upon you I have leaned from before my birth — you are he who took me from my mother's womb. My praise is continually of you.

7 I have been as a portent to many, but you are my strong refuge.

8 My mouth is filled with your praise, and with your glory all the day.

9 Do not cast me off in the time of old age — forsake me not when my strength is spent.

10 For my enemies speak concerning me — those who watch for my life consult together

11 And say, Creator has forsaken him — pursue and seize him, for there is none to deliver him.

12 O Creator, be not far from me — O my Creator, make haste to help me.

13 May my accusers be put to shame and consumed — with scorn and disgrace may those who seek my hurt be covered.

14 But I will hope continually and will praise you yet more and more.

15 My mouth will tell of your righteous acts, of your deeds of salvation all the day, for their number is past my knowledge.
