16 With the mighty deeds of YHWH Creator I will come — I will remind them of your righteousness, yours alone.

17 O Creator, from my youth you have taught me, and I still proclaim your wondrous deeds.

18 So even to old age and gray hairs, O Creator, do not forsake me, until I proclaim your might to another generation, your power to all those to come.

19 Your righteousness, O Creator, reaches the high heavens. You who have done great things, O Creator, who is like you?

20 You who have made me see many troubles and calamities will revive me again — from the depths of the earth you will bring me up again.

21 You will increase my greatness and comfort me again.

22 I will also praise you with the harp for your faithfulness, O my Creator — I will sing praises to you with the lyre, O Holy One of Yisra'EL.

23 My lips will shout for joy, when I sing praises to you — my soul also, which you have redeemed.

24 And my tongue will talk of your righteous help all the day long, for they have been put to shame and disappointed who sought to do me hurt.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 71 has no superscription and is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. It is a prayer of old age — the psalmist facing the vulnerability of advanced years while enemies wait for YHWH to abandon him. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 71 in full. The psalm is read as the covenant testimony of a lifetime: you have taught me from my youth — verse 17 — and the prayer that YHWH not forsake the psalmist in old age until the testimony has been passed to the next generation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 71. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — do not cast me off in the time of old age — forsake me not when my strength is spent. The enemies wait for exactly this moment — verses 10-11 — confident that YHWH has forsaken him. The vulnerability of old age is real: strength gone, the body failing. Verses 17-18 — from my youth you have taught me, and I still proclaim your wondrous deeds — so even to old age and gray hairs, do not forsake me, until I proclaim your might to another generation. The psalmist's prayer is not for comfort alone but for enough time to complete the transmission. The covenant knowledge must reach the next generation. The testimony must be passed on.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 72 Of Shlomo — Give the King Your Justice — May His Name Endure Forever — Closing Doxology of Book II

1 Give the king your justice, O Creator, and your righteousness to the royal son.

2 May he judge your people with righteousness, and your poor with justice.

3 Let the mountains bear prosperity for the people, and the hills, in righteousness.

4 May he defend the cause of the poor of the people, give deliverance to the children of the needy, and crush the oppressor.

5 May they fear you while the sun endures, and as long as the moon, throughout all generations.

6 May he be like rain that falls on the mown grass, like showers that water the earth.

7 In his days may the righteous flourish, and peace abound, till the moon be no more.

8 May he have dominion from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth.

9 May desert tribes bow down before him, and his enemies lick the dust.

10 May the kings of Tarshish and of the coastlands render him tribute — may the kings of Sheva and Seva bring gifts.

11 May all kings fall down before him, all nations serve him.

12 For he delivers the needy when he calls, the poor and him who has no helper.

13 He has pity on the weak and the needy, and saves the lives of the needy.

14 From oppression and violence he redeems their life, and precious is their blood in his sight.

15 Long may he live — may gold of Sheva be given to him — may prayer be made for him continually, and blessings invoked for him all the day.

16 May there be abundance of grain in the land — on the tops of the mountains may it wave — may its fruit be like Lebanon — and may people blossom in the cities like the grass of the field.

17 May his name endure forever, his fame continue as long as the sun — may people be blessed in him, all nations call him blessed.

18 Blessed be YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, who alone does wondrous things.

19 Blessed be his glorious name forever — may the whole earth be filled with his glory. Amen and Amen.

20 The prayers of Dawid, the son of Yishai, are ended.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 72 closes Book II of the Tehillim and is attributed to Shlomo. It is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The closing doxology of verses 18-19 and the editorial note of verse 20 — the prayers of Dawid the son of Yishai are ended — are preserved in the DSS tradition. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 72 in full including the closing doxology and editorial note. The psalm is read as both a royal prayer for the ideal king and a messianic vision of universal sovereignty through covenant justice.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 72. Verse 20 — the prayers of Dawid the son of Yishai are ended — is an ancient editorial note marking the close of a Davidic collection. It appears alongside the doxology that closes Book II. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12-14 — he delivers the needy when he calls — he has pity on the weak and the needy — from oppression and violence he redeems their life, and precious is their blood in his sight. The covenant king is defined not by military power but by his treatment of the poor. The standard of kingship in the Torah — Devarim 17:18-20 — is covenant faithfulness expressed in justice for the vulnerable. Verse 19 — Amen and Amen — the closing double Amen seals Book II as the double Amen of Psalm 41:13 sealed Book I. The entire first two books of the Tehillim close with the same covenant seal.
